items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.correct: Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).
items.why: Students assume 'three sections' means three different ones. Ternary has only TWO distinct ideas, but A returns - it's the return that makes it ternary, not the section count alone.

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.

items.q: The tabla is from which country?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: India. “Japan” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Tuned metal percussion. “Strings” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many notes per octave on a Western piano?
items.why: Exact answer: 12. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
